1.2: יִשָּׁקֵנִי (yishaqeni) — 'let him kiss me' is a jussive expressing longing desire, not a statement of fact. The Hebrew nashaq covers not only romantic kissing but the greeting kiss of family reunion and the covenant kiss (David and Jonathan, 1 Sam 20:41). דֹּדֶיךָ (dodekha) — 'your loves/your love' uses the same root dod that names the beloved throughout the Song. The MKT translates 'your love is better than wine' (singular), but the Hebrew is technically plural — the many expressions or instances of love. The comparison to wine (yayin) sets the standard of covenant celebration; wine gladdens the heart (Ps 104:15) and this beloved's love surpasses even that.
1.3: שֶׁמֶן (shemen) — anointing oil, also the word for richness/fertility. The connection between name (shem) and oil (shemen) in this verse is a deliberate paronomasia (wordplay): the name is like oil poured out — the same consonants (sh-m) in both words make the sound play audible in Hebrew. שְׁמֶךָ שֶׁמֶן מוּרָק (shimkha shemen muraq) = 'your name is like purified oil' — name and character are inseparable in Hebrew anthropology. The young women (alamot) who love him respond to his character-fragrance, not just his appearance.
1.4: מָשְׁכֵנִי (moshkeni) — 'draw me' uses the verb mashakh, which also describes the drawing of a sword (Ps 28:3) and the attraction of nets; it implies active pulling rather than mere beckoning. The sudden shift from 'let him' (third person, v2-3) to 'you' (second person direct address) is characteristic of the Song's fluid pronoun use — the beloved is sometimes addressed directly, sometimes spoken about. חֲדָרָיו (chadarav) — 'his chambers' — the inner room of the royal household; the most intimate space. The shift to first-person plural 'we will rejoice' includes the daughters of Jerusalem as witnesses.
1.5: שְׁחוֹרָה אֲנִי וְנָאוָה (shechorah ani venava'ah) — 'I am dark and lovely.' The conjunction is ve- (and), not but — the darkness and the loveliness are not in tension but simultaneous. Some translators import a contrast ('dark but lovely') that the Hebrew does not require. The similes — tents of Kedar (a nomadic Arab tribe whose black goat-hair tents were well known) and curtains of Solomon — pair the wild nomadic beauty with royal elegance. The darkness is the result of outdoor labor, not a deficiency; the beloved defends herself against social judgment.
1.6: כַּרְמִי שֶׁלִּי (karmi shelli) — 'my own vineyard' — the first-person emphatic reflexive. Throughout the Song the vineyard is a double image: literal (agricultural labor) and metaphorical (the body, sexuality). The brothers' anger (nibeharu — from bahar, to be hot, to flare) caused her to tend their vineyards at the expense of her own; she was compelled to outdoor labor while her personal vineyard was unattended. The 'vineyard I have not kept' sets up the thematic tension between external obligation and personal flourishing.
1.7: שֶׁאָהֲבָה נַפְשִׁי (she'ahavah nafshi) — 'the one my soul loves' — a phrase repeated five times in the Song (1:7; 3:1, 2, 3, 4). Nefesh here functions as the intensified self, the whole person's longing — not soul in the Greek dualistic sense but the totality of the person expressing desire. נוֹהֶה (noheh) — 'resting, grazing at midday' — the midday rest of shepherds in the shade was a practical necessity in the Near Eastern climate; the beloved asks not for romance but for practical information about where to find him.
1.8: הַיָּפָה בַּנָּשִׁים (hayafah bannashim) — 'the most beautiful of women' — the superlative in Hebrew is expressed with the article and comparison (literally 'the beautiful one among women'). The response addresses the woman as the most beautiful, not dismissing her practical question but answering it with an elevated address. צְאִי (tze'i) — 'go forth' — feminine imperative. The instruction to follow the tracks of the flock and pasture her own kids (gediyotayikh, feminine plural diminutive) mirrors the beloved's tending her brothers' vineyards — she who was forced to neglect her own now has her own flock to tend.
1.9: סוּסָתִי (susati) — specifically the feminine form, 'my mare.' The comparison is striking and culturally loaded: in Pharaoh's cavalry, mares were not used in battle — the Egyptians used stallions. An enemy's introduction of a mare into the battle horses could create chaos. The beloved's beauty is compared to a single mare among Pharaoh's stallions — the one who would captivate and throw the entire military formation into disruption. The comparison celebrates her power and magnetic attraction, not her domestication.
1.10: תֹּרִים (torim) — 'strings of beads' or 'pendants'; the root tor may relate to turtle-doves (similarly named in Hebrew) or to the geometric arrangement of jewelry. חֲרוּזִים (charuzim) — 'strings of beads' — from the root meaning to bore or pierce; the strung beads frame the face and neck. The jewelry description is not merely decorative; in the ANE, specific jewelry types indicated social status, marital status, and tribal affiliation. The beloved's jewelry adorns and places her.
1.11: נַעֲשֶׂה (na'aseh) — 'we will make' — the plural subject is the companions, attendants, or the chorus. The promise of gold ornaments studded with silver (nequdot hakesef — silver dots/studs) represents the social community's endorsement of the beloved; she is not simply desired by the beloved but acknowledged by the community around him. The plural 'we' includes the audience in the gift-giving.
1.12: בְּשַׁלְחָנוֹ (beshulchano) — 'at his table' (literally 'while at his table circling/reclining'). Meshivah — a variant reading for the dining posture. נֵרְדִּי (nerdi) — 'my nard' — the first-person possessive on the spikenard (the expensive imported aromatic) claims it as her own fragrance-identity. The nard perfume in response to the king's presence at table creates a sensory exchange: his presence draws out her fragrance.
1.13: צְרוֹר הַמֹּר (tzeror hamor) — 'a sachet of myrrh' — the term tzeror (bundle, pouch) is used for a bag of money (Gen 42:35; Prov 7:20) and a bundle of tied things. The myrrh (mor) was the most expensive imported resin from Arabia and Africa, used in anointing oil (Exod 30:23) and in burial spices. Its placement 'between my breasts' is the location of the myrrh sachet used medicinally and aromatically — and also the location of the beloved's heart. The beloved is between the body and the anointing of the sacred.
1.14: כֹּפֶר (kofer) — 'henna blossoms' — a hapax in this noun form, derived from the word for atonement/ransom (kaphar). The henna plant (Lawsonia inermis), used throughout the ancient Near East for dyeing and fragrance, grew abundantly at En-gedi. The wordplay between henna (kofer) and atonement (kippur) is likely intentional in a wisdom book: the beloved who is like a henna cluster from the place of fresh water by the Dead Sea carries a resonance of both beauty and sacred covering.
1.15: עֵינַיִךְ יוֹנִים (einayikh yonim) — 'your eyes are doves' — the dove (yonah) appears repeatedly in the Song (1:15; 2:12, 14; 4:1; 5:2, 12; 6:9). The dove's associations: gentleness, monogamy (dove pairs are faithful), the Spirit of God (Gen 8:8-12; Matt 3:16), and the sacrifice of the poor (Lev 5:7). The dove-eyes suggest purity, devotion, and gentle perceptiveness — not merely the color or shape but the character expressed in the eyes.
1.16: עַרְשֵׂנוּ (arsenu) — 'our bed' — the dual possession, signaled by the first-person plural suffix. The bed (eres) is not the marital bed exclusively but the resting place under the open sky — the bower of green (ra'anan) suggests a natural canopy of living branches. The house described in v17 with cedar beams and cypress rafters may be metaphorical — the forest itself as a house.
1.17: קוֹרוֹת בָּתֵּינוּ (korot bateinu) — 'the beams of our house' — cedar (erez) and cypress (berosh) were the most prestigious building materials in the ancient Near East; Solomon's temple was built of these (1 Kgs 6:15-16). The description of a house made of forest materials — or describing the forest itself as a house — suggests that the beloved and her companion inhabit both the palace and the natural world, or that the grandeur of their love surpasses even the royal building materials. The plural 'our' again asserts shared possession.

2.1: חֲבַצֶּלֶת הַשָּׁרוֹן (chavatzelet hasharon) — the specific identification of this flower is debated: the LXX translates as 'lily' (anthos), but other candidates include narcissus, meadow saffron, asphodel, or crocus. The Sharon (the coastal plain of Israel) was known for its lush vegetation; the valley lilies (shoshanat ha'amakim) appear in contrast to the cultivated highland gardens. The woman describes herself modestly — a common wildflower — before the beloved's response elevates her above all other flowers (v2).
2.2: כְּשׁוֹשַׁנָּה בֵּין הַחוֹחִים (keshoshanah bein hachochim) — 'like a lily among thorns' — the beloved has called herself a common wildflower; the lover responds with a pointed comparison that transforms her self-deprecation: she is not merely a flower but a lily among thorns — uniquely beautiful precisely by contrast with what surrounds her. הַחוֹחִים (hachochim) — 'thorns' uses the same root as the thorns of judgment in the prophets and Gen 3:18. The comparison elevates what she minimized.
2.3: כְּתַפּוּחַ בֵּין עֲצֵי הַיַּעַר (ke-tapuach bein atzei haya'ar) — 'like an apple tree among trees of the forest.' The identification of tapuach as apple is debated; the apricot and quince are also proposed (apples were not common in ancient Israel). What matters lexically is the contrast: a fruit-bearing, shade-providing, fragrant tree (taapuach is associated with fragrance, 7:9) among the non-productive forest trees — exactly mirroring the beloved's 'lily among thorns.' בְּצִלּוֹ (betzillo) — 'in his shade' — the shade of the tree as the beloved's dwelling; the protective shade is a covenant metaphor (Ps 17:8; 91:1: under the shadow of his wings).
2.4: בֵּית הַיַּיִן (beit hayayin) — 'house of wine' — the MKT renders 'banquet hall' which captures the social function: not simply a wine cellar but the room for celebratory drinking. דִּגְלוֹ עָלַי אַהֲבָה (diglo alay ahavah) — 'his banner over me was love' — degel (banner, standard) is a military term (Num 1:52; 2:2 — each tribe has its standard). The banner of love flying over the beloved claims her: she is under his flag, identified with him. The military image in an intimate context speaks of total claiming and total protection.
2.5: סַמְּכוּנִי (sammekuni) and רַפְּדוּנִי (rappeduni) — 'support me, refresh me' — both plural imperatives addressed to the daughters of Jerusalem. The beloved is overwhelmed by love — חוֹלַת אַהֲבָה (cholat ahavah) literally 'sick with love' — and she calls for physical support. The raisins (ashishot) and apples (tapuchim) as restoratives reflect the ancient use of dried fruit and cooling foods for recovery. The beloved's love-sickness is a motif in Egyptian love poetry; here it frames the complete absorption of the beloved in the relationship.
2.6: שְׂמֹאלוֹ (smolo) — his left arm; יְמִינוֹ (yemino) — his right arm (hand). The left-right embrace positions the beloved completely within the beloved's encompassing arms — surrounded, not merely touched. The right hand/arm in Hebrew idiom is the arm of strength and protection (Ps 89:13; Isa 41:10: I will uphold you with my righteous right hand). The full-body embrace in the Song is both intimate and protective: the beloved is covered by her beloved's strength.
2.7: מַשְׁבִּיעָה אֶתְכֶם (mashbia'ah etkhem) — 'I adjure you' — the verb shava' (to swear/adjure) is the same root as oath and the divine name Shaddai. The adjuration formula 'by the gazelles and the does of the field' (bitzevaot o bé'aylot hassadeh) is a wordplay: tzeviot (gazelles/does) sounds like Tzeviot (hosts, as in LORD of hosts, YHWH Tzeviot). The beloved adjures the daughters of Jerusalem by these creatures — either avoiding the divine name directly or invoking it through the euphemistic sound-alike. תָּעִירוּ (ta'iru) — 'rouse, stir up' — the love (ahavah) must come in its appointed time, not forced or hurried.
2.8: קוֹל דּוֹדִי (kol dodi) — 'the voice/sound of my beloved' — qol (sound, voice) is the first word of the verse; it announces before describing. מְדַלֵּג (medalleg) — 'leaping' — a Piel participle indicating vigorous, energetic movement; מְקַפֵּץ (meqapetz) — 'bounding' — a rarer verb also indicating quick movement. The beloved approaches with athletic energy, not sedately; the mountains and hills cannot slow him. The imagery anticipates theophany language (the LORD who 'comes leaping' appears in prophetic poetry).
2.9: מֵצִיץ מִן הַחַרַכִּים (meitzitz min hacharakim) — 'peering through the lattice windows' — the verb tzitz (to look through, to peer) shares a root with the priestly headpiece (ציץ, tzitz, the golden flower of the High Priest's mitre, Exod 28:36) and with the blossoming flower. The lover who peeps through the lattice is playfully active; the enclosed woman glimpses the one who seeks her through the screen that separates their spaces. The lattice windows of ancient Near Eastern homes provided both privacy and visibility.
2.10: עָנָה דוֹדִי (anah dodi) — 'my beloved spoke/responded' — the verb anah (answer, respond) implies that his speech is a response to an unspoken or implicit question. קוּמִי (qumi) — 'arise' — feminine imperative; לְכִי לָךְ (lekhi lakh) — 'come away for yourself' — the lakh (reflexive dative, for yourself) is idiomatic; it marks the invitation as for her own benefit and wholeness. The double command (arise, come away) parallels the Abrahamic call (Gen 12:1: go for yourself, lekh lekha) and the eschatological invitation.
2.11: הַסְּתָו עָבַר (hastav avar) — 'the winter is past' — stav (winter) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible as a season name; it may be a loanword. The perfect tense (avar = has passed) announces completed fact, not prediction. The rains (geshem) — the winter rains of Israel's agricultural calendar that came from October-March — have ended. The announcement has the character of a proclamation: the season of dormancy is definitively over. The covenant agricultural calendar is the background; the rains and dry season structured Israel's year.
2.12: הַנִּצָּנִים (hanietzanim) — 'the flowers/blossoms' — from the root netz, related to blossoming and sparkling (the same root gives the hawk its name for its sparkling flight). עֵת הַזָּמִיר (et hazamir) — 'the season of singing' — but zamir can mean both 'singing' and 'pruning' (of vines). The intentional ambiguity in the text allows both meanings: the season of vine-pruning and the season of song. The turtledove's voice (qol hator) — tor echoes Torah — marks the season as appointed and purposeful.
2.13: תְּאֵנָה חָנְטָה פַגֶּיהָ (te'enah chanetah faggeyha) — 'the fig tree is ripening its early figs' — the paggim (unripe early figs, lit. 'green figs') are the first fruit of the fig tree before the summer crop; their appearance marks late spring. The word chanetah (has spiced, has conditioned, has prepared) is unusual — normally the fig 'forms' its fruit; here it has prepared them. The vines (gefen) blossoming and spreading their fragrance completes the agricultural picture. The repeated invitation qumi... lekhi lakh (v13b = v10b) frames the natural description as an urgent summons.
2.14: יוֹנָתִי (yonati) — 'my dove' — the diminutive form of yonah (dove), carrying tender endearment. בְּחַגְוֵי הַסֶּלַע (bechagvei hasela) — 'in the clefts of the rock' — the rock and its clefts are places of divine protection (Exod 33:22: Moses placed in the cleft of the rock while God's glory passes; Isa 2:21: hiding in the clefts from divine judgment). The beloved hidden in the rock-cleft is protected and hidden; the lover desires to see her face and hear her voice — the very things that YHWH withholds at Sinai (Exod 33:20-23) but gives in covenant relationship.
2.15: שׁוּעָלִים (shu'alim) — 'foxes' — or possibly jackals; the same word covers both in biblical Hebrew. The complaint that small foxes ruin vineyards (while large predators are more visible) is a proverbial observation: small, cunning destructors often do more damage than obvious threats. The vineyard metaphor (established in 1:6) makes this a warning about what threatens the relationship: small, overlooked, cunning things that nibble at the growth. The instruction to 'catch the little foxes' is addressed to an unspecified plural — perhaps both lovers, perhaps the community.
2.16: דּוֹדִי לִי וַאֲנִי לוֹ (dodi li va'ani lo) — 'my beloved is mine and I am his' — the mutual-possession formula appears three times in the Song (2:16; 6:3; 7:10), each time with slightly different emphasis and word order. Here the beloved claims him first (dodi li = my beloved belongs to me); in 6:3 the order reverses (ani ledodi vedodi li = I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine); in 7:10 only her belonging to him is stated. Scholars note the progression as a deepening of her self-giving. הָרֹעֶה בַּשּׁוֹשַׁנִּים (haro'eh bashoshanim) — 'grazing among the lilies' — the beloved grazes in the lilies as a shepherd; both pastoral and intimate.
2.17: עַד שֶׁיָּפוּחַ הַיּוֹם (ad sheyafuach hayom) — 'until the day breathes/breaks' — pua (to breathe, to blow gently) is rare; it describes the gentle movement of the day's first breath of air. The phrase 'the day breathes' is a poetic description of dawn when the morning breeze stirs. נָסוּ הַצְּלָלִים (nasu hatzelalim) — 'and the shadows flee' — the shadows that persisted through the night give way to the first light. The invitation 'turn, my beloved' (sov demeh-lekha dodi) echoes the invitation to 'be like a gazelle or young stag' — the morning energy of the pursued/pursuing beloved closing the night-time distance.

3.1: בַּלֵּילוֹת (baleilot) — 'in the nights' — the plural is significant: this is not a single night but a recurring pattern of nighttime searching. The plural leilot suggests the anxious nights of longing rather than one specific occasion, giving the passage a dream-sequence or habitual quality. בִּקַּשְׁתִּיו (biqqashtihu) — 'I sought him' — the Piel intensive of baqash (to seek, to want urgently); the Piel intensifies the seeking. The phrase she'ahavah nafshi (the one my soul loves) is repeated three more times in this passage (vv2, 3, 4), creating a refrain of urgent longing.
3.2: אָקוּמָה נָּא (aqumah na) — 'let me arise, please' — the particle na marks the resolve as tentative, a persuasion of herself; she debates and then decides. אֲסוֹבְבָה (asobbevah) — 'I will go around/circuit' — the Polel of savav (to surround, to go around) suggests circling the city, searching all its circuits. בָּרְחֹבוֹת וּבַשְּׁוָקִים (barechobbot uvashvakim) — 'in the plazas and in the marketplaces' — the two public spaces of an ancient city: the broad open areas and the commercial streets. She will not hide her search in private but will make it public.
3.3: שֹׁמְרִים הַסֹּבְבִים בָּעִיר (shomrim hasovevim ba'ir) — 'the watchmen who circuit the city' — the same verb savav (to circuit) connects the beloved's search (v2: asobbevah) to the watchmen's circuit; she and they are doing the same action but for different purposes. The watchmen who encounter her do not arrest her (as in 5:7 where a later encounter is more violent); they are simply asked if they have seen the beloved. The question is not embarrassing; her public search for the one she loves is presented as urgent and appropriate.
3.4: כִּמְעַט שֶׁעָבַרְתִּי מֵהֶם (kim'at she'avarti mehem) — 'barely had I passed them' — the temporal clause expresses the speed of the finding; she had barely passed the watchmen when she found him. אָחַזְתִּיו (achaztihu) — 'I held him' — the Qal perfect with a possessive suffix; the holding is decisive, not tentative. לֹא אַרְפֶּנּוּ (lo arpennu) — 'I would not let him go' — a negative jussive expressing determination. The imagery of finding, holding, and bringing into the mother's house parallels Jacob's bringing Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent after their first meeting (Gen 24:67).
3.5: The refrain from 2:7 appears verbatim: I adjure you, daughters of Jerusalem, do not rouse love until it pleases. The reappearance of the adjuration after the finding (v4) — rather than after longing — suggests that love's timing is still the central concern even when love has been found: the mystery of love's movement cannot be controlled or forced even by those who have found it. The protective adjuration frames the entire dream-sequence.
3.6: מִי זֹאת עֹלָה מִן הַמִּדְבָּר (mi zot olah min hamidbar) — 'who is this coming up from the wilderness?' — the olah (coming up, ascending) is the same verb as the burnt offering (olah) and the pilgrimage ascent to Jerusalem (aliyah). The wilderness-to-city ascent is the Exodus pattern; this procession is fragrant with myrrh and frankincense and all the merchant's powders — the full array of the incense trade. כְּתִימְרוֹת עָשָׁן (ke-timrot ashan) — 'like columns of smoke' — the incense-smoke of worship ascending; the column of cloud in the Exodus narrative.
3.7: מִטָּתוֹ שֶׁלִּשְׁלֹמֹה (mitato shelishlomoh) — 'the litter/bed of Solomon' — mittah can mean a bed or a portable litter (a carrying bed for royalty). The identification of Solomon introduces the royal dimension that recurs in ch3-4. Sixty warriors surround the litter — the number sixty appears in military contexts throughout the ancient Near East (Ugaritic texts describe sixty heroes; the Psalms use sixty as a military round number).
3.8: אֲחֻזֵי חֶרֶב (achuzei cherev) — 'grasping/holding a sword' — the warriors are not merely armed but actively gripping their weapons, at constant readiness. מְלֻמְּדֵי מִלְחָמָה (melummedei milchamah) — 'trained in battle' — the Pual participle indicates formal training, professional soldiers rather than militia. מִפַּחַד בַּלֵּילוֹת (mippachad baleilot) — 'for fear in the nights' — the same plural 'nights' as v1; the night that is the realm of the beloved's searching is also the realm of danger requiring armed guard.
3.9: אַפִּרְיוֹן (apiryon) — 'royal litter/palanquin' — a hapax legomenon (used only here in the Hebrew Bible). Its etymology is disputed: some derive it from Sanskrit paryanka (couch) via Persian; others from Egyptian; still others from Greek phoreion (a portable chair). The foreign loanword emphasizes the exotic luxury of Solomon's royal vehicle. מֵעֲצֵי הַלְּבָנוֹן (me'atzei hallevanon) — 'from Lebanese cedar' — the premium building material, as in the Temple (1 Kgs 5:6-10). The palanquin is built to temple standards.
3.10: עַמּוּדָיו עָשָׂה כֶסֶף (ammudav asah kesef) — 'its posts he made of silver' — the progressive enumeration silver (posts), gold (back/canopy), purple (seat), matches the order of materials used in the Tabernacle (Exod 26-27: the progression from outer court to the Most Holy Place uses silver, gold, and purple in ascending order of holiness). The interior of the palanquin is lined (murchav ahavah) — the MKT has 'lovingly lined by the daughters of Jerusalem' — but the Hebrew is difficult; rachav (paved/laid) with love, or love as the material.
3.11: צְאֶינָה (tze'enah) — 'come out, go forth' — feminine plural imperative addressed to 'daughters of Zion' — the first appearance of Tzion (Zion) rather than Yerushalayim (Jerusalem) in the Song. The shift to Zion carries the full weight of the covenant city. עֲטֶרֶת (atarah) — crown — the same word used for the crown of rejoicing (1 Thess 2:19) and the crown placed on Job (Job 31:36). The crown given by the mother (immo) at the wedding day signals that the mother's role in the wedding investiture is recognized, unusual in ancient Near Eastern contexts where the father typically crowns the king.

4.1: כֻּלָּךְ יָפָה — this phrase appears in v7 as the full statement; in v1 the description builds toward it. מִבַּעַד לְצַמָּתֵךְ (miba'ad letzammatekh) — 'behind your veil' — tzammah is usually rendered 'veil' or 'locks of hair'; the image is either of the eyes visible through the veil or the eyes framed by the hair. The flock of goats descending from Gilead (hehar hagil'ad) — Gilead was the trans-Jordanian highland known for its goat herds; the comparison of flowing dark hair to a flock descending the hillside is a visual metaphor for movement, abundance, and dark color.
4.2: כְּעֵדֶר הַקְּצוּבוֹת (ke'eder haqetzuvot) — 'like a flock of freshly shorn sheep' — the teeth compared to freshly washed sheep emerge from the water clean, bright, and perfectly matched (shekulam taamot — all matched in pairs). שַׁכֻּלָה (shokulah) — 'bereaved/missing one' — from the root for childlessness or bereavement; the point is that not one tooth is missing, just as no ewe is without its twin. The unusual vocabulary of grief (shokulan) in a beauty description creates a poignant contrast: nothing is lost, nothing is missing.
4.3: חוּט הַשָּׁנִי (chut hashani) — 'thread of scarlet' — the scarlet thread (shani) appears in the Tabernacle curtains (Exod 25:4) and in Rahab's scarlet cord (Josh 2:18) — a sign of protection and covenant mark. The beloved's lips are like this covenant-marker thread. מִדְבָּרֵךְ (midbarekh) — 'your mouth' — literally 'your speech/word'; the same root as midbar (wilderness) but also meaning word, speech. The beloved's speech is lovely (naveh).
4.4: מִגְדַּל דָּוִיד (migdal David) — 'the tower of David' — the specific identification of the architectural reference. The tower built in courses (talpiyot — possibly a technical term for courses of stone, or a place name) with a thousand shields hung on it — all the shields of the warriors. The neck like a tower bearing shields is a striking metaphor: the beloved's neck carries the weapons that protect — the strength and dignity of her bearing becomes a military image of both beauty and power.
4.5: שְׁנֵי שָׁדַיִךְ (shnei shadayikh) — 'your two breasts' — shad (breast) also relates to the divine name El Shaddai (God Almighty), connected by some scholars to the root shad (to nourish, to give milk). The comparison to twin gazelle fawns (te'ome tzevi — twins of a gazelle) feeding among the lilies suggests nourishing abundance, matched proportion, and delicate beauty simultaneously.
4.6: הַר הַמֹּר (har hamor) — 'the mountain of myrrh' — a unique geographic reference; it may be the Temple Mount (associated with myrrh in the incense), a metaphorical description of the beloved's body, or an actual hill. גִּבְעַת הַלְּבוֹנָה (givat halevonah) — 'the hill of frankincense' — the two most sacred incense ingredients of the Temple offering (Exod 30:34). The beloved's body is compared to the mountain of sacred incense — the place where heaven and earth meet in the fragrance of worship.
4.7: כֻּלָּךְ יָפָה רַעְיָתִי (kullakh yafah ra'yati) — 'you are altogether beautiful, my love' — kullakh (all of you, entirely you) is the emphatic totality; every part just described is summed in the whole-person declaration. וּמוּם אֵין בָּךְ (umum ein bakh) — 'and there is no defect in you' — mum is a technical term from the Levitical purity code for the blemishes that disqualify animals for sacrifice and priests for service (Lev 21:17-23; 22:17-25). The declaration of no blemish uses the priestly vocabulary of sacrificial perfection: the beloved is entirely suitable, without disqualifying mark.
4.8: אִתִּי מִלְּבָנוֹן כַּלָּה (itti millevanon kallah) — 'with me from Lebanon, O bride' — the first use of kallah (bride) in the Song; it appears six times in ch4-5 as the primary address for the beloved. Kallah means both 'bride' and 'daughter-in-law' (from kalal, to complete, to perfect). The invitation to come down from Lebanon — from the mountain peaks of Amana, Senir, and Hermon (the northern frontier) — is an invitation from the wild heights to the intimate home.
4.9: לִבַּבְתִּנִי (libbavtini) — 'you have captivated/stolen my heart' — a denominative verb from lev (heart), occurring only here in the Hebrew Bible. It is a newly coined verb meaning 'you have given me a heart attack' or 'you have taken/captured my heart.' The Piel stem (libbavtini) implies the beloved has actively done this to the lover, not accidentally. The doubling in v9 — 'with one of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace' — emphasizes that a single glance has the full effect; she does not need to deploy all her beauty.
4.10: מַה יָּפוּ דֹדַיִךְ (mah yafu dodayikh) — 'how beautiful is your love' — the echo of the opening verse (1:2: your love is better than wine) is explicit; the beloved now mirrors the language back. מֵיַּיִן (meyayin) — 'than wine' — the same comparison as 1:2 but now the beloved speaks it to the woman, not the woman speaking it of the man. The reciprocity of the Song's language is intentional: the same terms of love apply equally to both.
4.11: נֹפֶת תִּטֹּפְנָה שִׂפְתוֹתַיִךְ (nofet titofnah siftotayikh) — 'your lips drip with nectar' — nofet (nectar, honeycomb drippings) is the finest form of honey, the free-dripping liquid from the comb. תַּחַת לְשׁוֹנֵךְ (tachat leshonekh) — 'under your tongue' — honey and milk under the tongue; the physical location of the sweetest taste. The fragrance of her garments compared to Lebanon connects to the cedar forests whose scent was proverbially fresh and pure.
4.12: גַּן נָעוּל (gan na'ul) — 'a locked garden' — the triple description: locked garden, sealed spring, closed fountain all speak of enclosure and exclusivity. The participle na'ul (locked, bolted) uses the same root as the bolt on a door (Neh 3:3). In the ANE, the enclosed garden was both a symbol of paradise (the Persian pairideaza = walled garden = paradise) and of the protected woman in marriage. The locked garden is not imprisonment but exclusive dedication: she is a garden that belongs entirely to one.
4.13: שְׁלָחַיִךְ (shelachayikh) — 'your branches/shoots' — from shalach (to send out, extend); the beloved's body is described as an orchard with extending branches. The catalogue of spices and fruits — pomegranates (rimmonim), henna (kopher), nard (nerd) — parallels the incense-spice lists of Exodus 30 and the merchant-spice inventories of the ancient Near East. The woman's body is a garden of the most precious aromatic commodities of the ancient trade routes.
4.14: The extended spice list continues: nard and saffron (karkom), calamus (qaneh) and cinnamon (qinnamon), frankincense trees (atzei levonah), myrrh (mor) and aloes (ahalot) — the precise vocabulary of the incense trade. Several of these plants (calamus, cinnamon, cassia) appear in the holy anointing oil formula of Exod 30:23-25. The beloved is simultaneously a personal garden and a catalogue of the sacred. The MKT's 'all the finest spices' captures the superlative quality.
4.15: מַעְיַן גַּנִּים (ma'yan gannim) — 'a spring/fountain of gardens' — the same term as v12's sealed spring, now opened and flowing. The imagery shifts from enclosure (locked, sealed) to outpouring (fountain, streams). נֹזְלִים מִן לְבָנוֹן (nozlim min levanon) — 'streams flowing from Lebanon' — the melting snows of Lebanon fed the Jordan and the coastal rivers; fresh water from the mountains was the purest and most life-giving. The beloved as a mountain spring connects to the wilderness imagery (3:6: coming up from the wilderness) and the living water of prophetic promise (Isa 58:11).
4.16: עוּרִי צָפוֹן וּבוֹאִי תֵימָן (uri tzafon uvoi teiman) — 'awake, O north wind; come, O south wind' — the beloved summons the two winds to blow through her garden. The north and south winds bracket the entire compass; she calls the full range of natural forces to carry her fragrance to her beloved. יָבֹא דוֹדִי לְגַנּוֹ (yavo dodi legano) — 'let my beloved come to his garden' — she has already made the shift from 'my garden' to 'his garden'; the gift of herself precedes the union of ch5:1.

5.1: בָּאתִי לְגַנִּי (bati leganni) — 'I have come to my garden' — the immediate fulfillment of the beloved's invitation at the end of ch4. The Qal perfect marks completed action. The fivefold 'I gathered / I ate / I drank / I ate / I drank' follows the chiastic structure of invitation and response. The final summons — 'eat, friends; drink and be intoxicated, lovers (dodim)' — addresses a wider audience: the friends are invited to share the joy of the union.
5.2: אֲנִי יְשֵׁנָה וְלִבִּי עֵר (ani yeshenah velibbi er) — 'I slept but my heart was awake' — the paradox of the dream-state or half-waking. Libbi er (my heart is awake/watching) uses the same root as the sentinel who guards (ir = watchman, city). The heart that watches while the body sleeps is the deepest level of longing — consciousness at rest but the inner person alert. דּוֹפֵק (dofek) — the participle 'knocking' describes continuous, repeated knocking, not a single knock.
5.3: פָּשַׁטְתִּי אֶת כֻּתָּנְתִּי (pashatti et kuttaneti) — 'I have taken off my robe' — the excuse uses the same logic as the foot-washing hesitation: the domestic ritual of removing and putting on is used to express the cost and awkwardness of re-dressing. Kuttonet (robe, inner garment) is the same word used for Joseph's robe (Gen 37:3) and the priestly garments (Exod 28:4). The beloved's reluctance is tenderly comic; the lover does not force entry but calls and waits.
5.4: שָׁלַח יָדוֹ מִן הַחֹר (shalach yado min hachor) — 'he thrust his hand through the latch opening' — chor (hole, opening) is the slot in the door for the bolt. The beloved's insertion of his hand through the lock-opening is the most intimate physical action of seeking entry. מֵעַי הָמוּ עָלָיו (me'ai hamu alav) — 'my inmost being stirred for him' — me'ai (bowels, inward parts) is the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology (Gen 43:30; Isa 63:15). The MKT's 'deep longing' captures the visceral nature.
5.5: מֹר עֹבֵר (mor over) — 'flowing/passing myrrh' — the over (flowing, overflowing) participle describes myrrh that drips spontaneously rather than deliberately applied. Her hands and fingers drip with myrrh that the beloved has left on the door handle — his perfume has transferred to the latch he touched, then to her hands when she opens the door. The fragrance trail is the only trace of his recent presence.
5.6: נַפְשִׁי יָצְאָה בְדַבְּרוֹ (nafshi yatza'ah bedabbero) — 'my soul went out at his speaking' — the idiom yatza nefesh (the soul goes out) is used in Genesis 35:18 for Rachel's death (her soul going out). Here it captures the near-fainting intensity of the beloved's response to his voice at the moment she has just missed him. בִּקַּשְׁתִּיהוּ וְלֹא מְצָאתִיהוּ (biqqashtihu velo metzatihu) — 'I sought him but did not find him' — the searching of 3:1-4 is repeated but without the finding.
5.7: מְצָאֻנִי הַשֹּׁמְרִים (metzauni hashomrim) — 'the watchmen found me' — contrast with 3:3 where they were encountered and questioned; here the watchmen's action is violent. הִכּוּנִי פְצָעוּנִי (hikkuni fetzauni) — 'they beat me, they wounded me' — the violence of the watchmen in this encounter has generated much discussion; the beloved's search in the night streets provokes a defensive reaction from those charged with maintaining order. נְשָׂאוּ אֶת רְדִידִי (nasau et redidi) — 'they took my veil' — stripping the veil was a public shaming gesture (Num 5:18; Isa 47:2-3).
5.8: The third and final instance of the adjuration formula to the daughters of Jerusalem, but now with a new content: if they find the beloved, tell him she is חוֹלַת אַהֲבָה (cholat ahavah) — 'sick/faint with love.' This phrase brackets the central section: it appeared first in 2:5 (she was faint with love while in the beloved's arms); now she is faint while separated from him. The same condition, different circumstances, connects the two scenes.
5.9: The daughters of Jerusalem ask the theological question of the wasf: what makes your beloved different from all others? מַה דּוֹדֵךְ מִדּוֹד (mah dodekh midad) — 'what is your beloved more than another beloved?' — the comparative uses the same dod root: what makes your dod better than anyone else's dod? The question sets up the extended praise of the beloved in vv10-16 — the most elaborate description of a man's beauty in the Hebrew Bible.
5.10: צַח וְאָדוֹם (tzach ve'adom) — 'radiant and ruddy' — tzach (dazzling, glowing, pure white) paired with adom (red, ruddy) creates the color portrait of the beloved: both shining and warm-toned. David is described as admoni (ruddy) in 1 Sam 16:12. דָּגוּל מֵרְבָבָה (dagul meirevavah) — 'outstanding among ten thousand' — degel (banner/standard) reappears in a verbal form: he is 'bannered above ten thousand' — raised like a standard above a multitude. The MKT's 'outstanding' captures the military imagery.
5.11: קְוֻצּוֹתָיו תַּלְתַּלִּים (qevutzotav taltallim) — 'his hair is wavy/hanging in waves' — taltallim is a rare word, possibly from the root for hanging/dropping; the MKT's 'wavy' suggests the undulating quality. שְׁחֹרוֹת כָּעוֹרֵב (shechorot ka'orev) — 'dark as a raven' — the raven (orev) is jet black; its blackness is the standard for the darkest dark in Hebrew idiom.
5.12: עֵינָיו כְּיוֹנִים (einav keyonim) — 'his eyes are like doves' — the dove-eye comparison is now applied to the beloved (previously in 1:15 and 4:1 it described the woman's eyes). The dove eyes beside streams of water (afikei mayim) — bathed in milk (rechatzot bechalav), perfectly set (yoshevot al millat) — describe clarity, brightness, and perfect positioning. The eyes that are 'sitting on fullness' (mil'at = fullness/setting) suggests eyes that are complete and satisfied.
5.13: לְחָיָיו כַּעֲרוּגַת הַבֹּשֶׂם (lechayav ka'arugat habosem) — 'his cheeks are like a bed of spices' — arugah (garden bed, planted section) is a technical agricultural term for a raised planting bed for herbs. The cheeks are fragrant garden plots. שִׂפְתוֹתָיו שׁוֹשַׁנִּים (siftotav shoshanim) — 'his lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh' — the lilies and flowing myrrh combine the floral and the aromatic; his speech is like lilies dipped in myrrh.
5.14: יָדָיו גְּלִילֵי זָהָב (yadav gelilei zahav) — 'his hands are cylinders/rollers of gold' — gelil (cylinder, roller) is the same word used for the rolled curtain-rods of the Tabernacle (Exod 26:29). The hands are like the golden rods that hold the sacred curtains — both functional and ornamental. בְּטֶן עַשֶּׁת שֵׁן (beten asheth shen) — 'his torso is polished ivory' — shen (ivory/tooth) was the luxury material for palace furnishings (1 Kgs 22:39; Amos 6:4).
5.15: שׁוֹקָיו עַמּוּדֵי שֵׁשׁ (shoqav ammudei shesh) — 'his legs are columns of alabaster/marble' — shesh (alabaster, fine marble, or Egyptian linen of the same name) creates pillars of smooth, white stone. מֻיָּסָדִים עַל אַדְנֵי פָז (meyusadim al adnei faz) — 'set on sockets of finest gold' — adnim (sockets/bases) is the same technical term used for the silver sockets of the Tabernacle (Exod 26:19-25). The beloved's legs as Tabernacle pillars on golden bases places the praise of the beloved in the vocabulary of sacred architecture.
5.16: חִכּוֹ מַמְתַּקִּים (chikko mamtaqim) — 'his mouth is sweetnesses' — the plural mamtaqim expresses the superlative or the multiple kinds of sweetness; his mouth is all sweetnesses. וְכֻלּוֹ מַחֲמַדִּים (vekullo machamadim) — 'and he is entirely desirable' — machamad (desirable, coveted thing) is the same word used for the desirable things of the Temple (Lam 1:10; Isa 64:11). The beloved is entirely a machamad — the most desirable thing imaginable. The final identification: zeh dodi vezeh re'i (this is my beloved and this is my friend) — the beloved is not only the object of erotic love but a re'ah, a companion and friend.

6.1: The daughters of Jerusalem's question — where has your beloved gone? — mirrors 3:1-4 but at a more advanced stage: then the beloved searched alone; now she has companions who offer to search with her. Their offer to search with her (nevaqshennu immakh) uses the Piel plural — intensive, communal seeking. The community's inclusion in the search is a social affirmation of the beloved's love as something the community endorses and participates in.
6.2: לְגַנּוֹ (legano) — 'to his garden' — the beloved knows exactly where her beloved has gone because she IS his garden (4:12-16; 5:1). The answer to the daughters' question is reflexive: he has gone to tend her. לִרְעוֹת בַּגַּנִּים (lir'ot bagganim) — 'to graze in the gardens' — the shepherd-beloved grazes among the lilies; both the shepherd metaphor and the garden metaphor resolve in the same place. לִלְקֹט שׁוֹשַׁנִּים (lilqot shoshanim) — 'to gather lilies' — the gathering of lilies echoes the beloved gathering spices (5:1); the action is complete and intimate.
6.3: אֲנִי לְדוֹדִי וְדוֹדִי לִי (ani ledodi vedodi li) — 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' — the order reverses from 2:16 (dodi li va'ani lo — my beloved is mine and I am his). The earlier formulation emphasized her claim on him; this formulation emphasizes her belonging to him before her claim on him. Scholars note the order's significance: maturation in the Song moves from self-assertion to self-giving. The phrase expresses the same mutual covenant formula but with a subtle deepening.
6.4: כְּתִרְצָה (ketirtzah) — 'beautiful as Tirzah' — Tirzah was the capital of the northern kingdom before Samaria (1 Kgs 15:33; 16:6), known for its beauty. The comparison places the beloved among the greatest cities of Israel. כִּירוּשָׁלַיִם (kiyerushalayim) — 'lovely as Jerusalem' — Jerusalem as the standard of civic beauty. אֲיֻמָּה כַּנִּדְגָּלוֹת (ayumah kannidgalot) — 'awe-inspiring as an army with banners' — ayom (awe-inspiring, terrible) describes the beloved's effect on observers: not merely beautiful but commanding, creating a mixture of attraction and reverent awe.
6.5: הַשִּׁיבִי עֵינַיִךְ מִנֶּגְדִּי (hashivi einayikh minnegdi) — 'look away; your eyes overwhelm me' — the same dove-eyes that were praised (1:15; 4:1) now overwhelm the beloved. Rahavuni (they overwhelm me) from the root rahav (to press, overwhelm, behave proudly/arrogantly against); the eyes have a power that the lover finds too much to receive directly. The repetition of the hair-goats-Gilead comparison (= 4:1) signals intentional refrain-use.
6.6: The repetition of the teeth comparison (4:2 verbatim) emphasizes the refrain-structure of the wasf sequences. The beloved's beauty is consistently described in the same terms — the repetition is not laziness but the artistic convention of the ancient love lyric: the repeated image becomes the signature description, as familiar as a beloved's name.
6.7: The pomegranate-cheeks comparison also repeats from 4:3. The pomegranate (rimmon) was a symbol of fertility and abundance throughout the ancient Near East; its bright red interior suggests the warmth visible through the veil. The architectural detail (behind your veil, mibba'ad letzammatekh) frames the description in the same modest partially-revealed way.
6.8: שִׁשִּׁים הֵמָּה מְלָכוֹת (shishim hemah melakhot) — 'there may be sixty queens' — the numerical catalogue (sixty queens, eighty concubines, maidens without number) creates the social context against which the beloved is set. The numbers are not exact but rhetorical: a multitude of beautiful women whom a king might possess cannot compare with the uniquely valued one. The contrast anticipates the declaration of uniqueness in v9.
6.9: אַחַת הִיא יוֹנָתִי תַמָּתִי (achat hi yonati tammati) — 'she is unique — my dove, my perfect one' — achat (one, unique) is the declaration of incomparable singularity: she is the only one. Tammah (perfect, complete, whole) uses the same root as tom (integrity, wholeness) and tam (blameless, like Job). The beloved is declared perfect with the vocabulary of moral and ritual integrity. אַחַת הִיא לְאִמָּהּ (achat hi le'immah) — 'she is the only one of her mother' — her uniqueness is confirmed by her family origin.
6.10: מִי זֹאת (mi zot) — 'who is this?' — echoes 3:6 and 8:5; the 'who is this?' question is the Song's way of marking the beloved's appearance as an event, an arrival that demands attention and identification. כַּשַּׁחַר (kashachar) — 'like the dawn' — shachar (dawn) is the beginning of light breaking through darkness; the beloved's appearance is compared to the first light of day. כַּחַמָּה (kechamah) — 'like the sun' — the full sun; כַּלְּבָנָה (kallevanah) — 'like the moon' — the full moon. Dawn, moon, sun — the complete range of luminaries — describe the beloved's radiance.
6.11: אֶל גִּנַּת אֱגוֹז יָרַדְתִּי (el ginnat egoz yaradti) — 'I went down to the walnut grove' — the only occurrence of egoz (walnut) in the Hebrew Bible. The walnut grove is a cultivated garden space; the going down (yarad) is a descent, perhaps to the lower garden terraces. לִרְאוֹת בְּאִבֵּי הַנָּחַל (lirot be'ibbei hanachal) — 'to look at the new growth in the valley' — ibbei (blossoms, new growth) from the root for budding; the valley's spring growth and the vineyard's blossoming are the evidence of the season's fruitfulness.
6.12: לֹא יָדַעְתִּי (lo yada'ti) — 'I did not know' — the sudden surprise of the verse; the speaker did not realize what was happening. This is one of the most difficult verses in the Song: the Hebrew nafshi samAtni markevot ammi nadiv is grammatically complex and debated. Translations range from 'before I realized it, my desire had set me among the chariots of my noble people' to 'I did not know it, but my soul had made me like the chariots of Amminadab.' The ambiguity may be intentional: the beloved is overcome by something she cannot fully articulate — desire placing her somewhere she did not consciously choose to be.
6.13: שׁוּבִי שׁוּבִי הַשּׁוּלַמִּית (shuvi shuvi hashulammit) — 'come back, come back, O Shulamite' — the only occurrence of Shulamit in the Song. Its meaning is debated: (1) a woman from Shunem (the town of Abishag, 1 Kgs 1:3); (2) the feminine form of Solomon (Shelomoh), making her 'the Solomonic one' or 'the peaceful one'; (3) a term meaning 'the complete/whole one' (shalam = wholeness/peace). The quadruple shuvi (return, return, return, return) is an urgent, tender summons. The response question — why do you gaze on the Shulamite, as on the dance of the two camps? — introduces the dance imagery of ch7.
